description:
A compliance officer has asked you to gather the exact values of three
    mandatory configuration keys from every INI file shipped with an
    internal application suite.

    Files that must be audited:
      • /home/user/configs/app1.ini  
      • /home/user/configs/app2.ini

    Keys that must be captured (all are under the [General] section of every
    file):
      1. ComplianceLevel
      2. Encryption
      3. Version

    What you must do
      1. Parse the two INI files listed above – do NOT hard-code any of the
         values; read them from the files themselves.
      2. Create a new directory called /home/user/audit if it does not
         already exist.
      3. Inside that directory, create a file named audit_report.log that
         contains exactly one line for each key found (there will be six
         lines in total: 3 keys × 2 files).

    Required line format (single line, no spaces):
         FILE:<absolute-path>|SECTION:General|KEY:<key-name>|VALUE:<value>

         Example of the exact syntax and delimiter usage
           FILE:/some/path/example.ini|SECTION:General|KEY:Encryption|VALUE:AES256

    Ordering requirements
      • First sort by the INI filename in alphabetical order
        (/home/user/configs/app1.ini comes before app2.ini).
      • Then, for each file, list the keys in the following fixed order:
          ComplianceLevel, Encryption, Version

    Verification notes
      • /home/user/audit/audit_report.log MUST exist once you are finished.
      • It MUST contain exactly six lines and nothing else.
      • Every line must strictly follow the format shown above, including
        upper-case field names and the ‘|’ delimiter.
      • The compliance team will run an automated test that reads the INI
        files and compares the extracted values with those in
        audit_report.log, so pay close attention to accuracy.

    Hint
      This can be accomplished with only a couple of standard shell
      utilities (e.g., grep, awk, or sed) and basic directory/file commands,
      but you may use any non-interactive tools available in a typical
      Debian/Ubuntu environment.
truth:
Initial files on disk BEFORE the agent starts:

    1. /home/user/configs/app1.ini
       ┌─ file contents ─┐
       [General]
       Version=2.0
       ComplianceLevel=PCI-DSS
       Encryption=AES256

       [Network]
       Port=443
       Protocol=HTTPS
       └─────────────────┘

    2. /home/user/configs/app2.ini
       ┌─ file contents ─┐
       [General]
       Version=1.4
       ComplianceLevel=HIPAA
       Encryption=AES128

       [Network]
       Port=8443
       Protocol=HTTPS
       └─────────────────┘

    Expected file AFTER successful task completion:

    3. /home/user/audit/audit_report.log
       ┌─ file contents ─┐
       FILE:/home/user/configs/app1.ini|SECTION:General|KEY:ComplianceLevel|VALUE:PCI-DSS
       FILE:/home/user/configs/app1.ini|SECTION:General|KEY:Encryption|VALUE:AES256
       FILE:/home/user/configs/app1.ini|SECTION:General|KEY:Version|VALUE:2.0
       FILE:/home/user/configs/app2.ini|SECTION:General|KEY:ComplianceLevel|VALUE:HIPAA
       FILE:/home/user/configs/app2.ini|SECTION:General|KEY:Encryption|VALUE:AES128
       FILE:/home/user/configs/app2.ini|SECTION:General|KEY:Version|VALUE:1.4
       └─────────────────┘
name: task_000000_64e19572
